techniques.name: Swaddle
techniques.whenToUse: Any time baby is fussy, especially in the first 6–8 weeks when the Moro reflex is strong
steps: Lay a square muslin flat, folded into a diamond. Fold the top corner down about 15 cm
Place baby with shoulders at the folded edge — body below, head above
Take one arm, lay it straight down or slightly bent, wrap that side of the blanket snugly across baby's chest and tuck firmly behind their back
Fold the bottom corner up over baby's feet — hips must be able to move freely (frog-leg position is fine)
Bring the remaining side across the front and tuck in securely
Check: you can fit 2 fingers between blanket and baby's chest, but it won't unravel when lifted
tips: Use a velcro swaddle wrap (Halo, Love to Dream) when you're too tired to fold — they stay put and are faster
Stop swaddling as soon as baby shows any sign of attempting to roll (usually 8–16 weeks)
A swaddle only works if baby can't break out of it — practice the technique in the daytime first

techniques.name: Shush & Pat
techniques.whenToUse: Baby is fussy or overtired but not at peak cry — works well in the crib to avoid picking up
steps: Place baby on their side (supervised only) or pat through the crib with baby on their back
Put your mouth close to baby's ear and make a loud 'shhhhhh' — louder than the crying
Simultaneously pat the upper back or bum rhythmically — firm and steady, like a heartbeat
Maintain the shush-pat together: both the sound and the touch
Gradually reduce the intensity as baby calms — slow the pat, lower the shush
Stop patting once baby is calm — too much stimulation keeps them awake
tips: The shush needs to be genuinely loud — a quiet shush does nothing when baby is crying
Rhythm matters more than force — a metronomic pat is more effective than a random one
This technique requires a few minutes of persistence — it doesn't work in 5 seconds

techniques.name: Skin-to-Skin
techniques.whenToUse: Fussy, overstimulated, gassy, or just needing to regulate — works at any age in the early months
steps: Remove baby's clothes down to the nappy
Open your shirt and hold baby against your bare chest
Cover you both with a blanket for warmth
Let baby hear your heartbeat — the familiar sound is calming
Breathe slowly and deeply — babies co-regulate with your nervous system
tips: Works for dads and partners too — not just mum
If baby is overstimulated, skin-to-skin in a quiet dark room is one of the most effective resets
Also excellent for building supply when breastfeeding — prolactin rises during skin-to-skin

techniques.name: Rhythmic Motion (Sway & Jiggle)
techniques.whenToUse: Baby is at peak cry — vigorous rhythmic motion is often the fastest way to engage the calming reflex
steps: Hold baby securely in a slightly upright position, supporting the head
Sway side-to-side in a smooth, continuous rhythm (like a metronome)
For very upset babies, add a gentle jiggle — about 1–2 times per second, like you're shaking a salad
Match the intensity of the motion to the intensity of the cry — faster when they're more upset, slow down as they calm
Once calm, slow to a gentle rock and eventually still
tips: In-arms swaying is more effective than a swing for a peak-cry baby — the physical connection matters
Walking in a figure-8 pattern while swaying provides more variation and can work when simple swaying doesn't
Keep your own breathing steady — stressed parents transmit their state to baby

techniques.name: White Noise
techniques.whenToUse: At sleep time, during fussy periods, or as a constant background to mask household sounds
steps: Use a white noise machine, phone app, or fan placed at least 2 metres from baby
Set volume to about 65–70 dB — similar to a running shower (loud enough to hear over light crying)
For sleeping: leave it on for the full sleep period (not just to fall asleep)
For calming: bring the sound close and louder to match the crying, then gradually reduce as baby calms
tips: Fan noise, 'brown' noise, and 'pink' noise are often more effective than white noise for some babies
Avoid music or nature sounds with distinct starts and stops — the gaps cause waking
Consistent white noise throughout sleep helps mask transitions between sleep cycles
Check your machine's volume at baby's ear level — many machines are louder than they sound at a distance

techniques.name: Non-Nutritive Sucking
techniques.whenToUse: Baby needs comfort between feeds, during nappy changes, after a cry — once breastfeeding is established
steps: Offer a pacifier — try a few shapes to find what baby accepts
Or offer your clean pinky finger, nail-side down, at the roof of their mouth
Allow baby to suck at their own rhythm — don't pump the finger/dummy
Use in combination with swaddling and white noise for maximum effect
tips: Introduce pacifier after breastfeeding is established (3–4 weeks) to avoid nipple confusion
The sucking reflex is one of the strongest calming mechanisms — use it
Don't force the pacifier in if baby spits it out repeatedly — some babies never take one and that's okay

techniques.name: Tummy Massage (Gas Relief)
techniques.whenToUse: Baby seems gassy, uncomfortable after feeds, pulling up legs, or has a hard tummy
steps: Lay baby on their back on a firm, flat surface
Warm your hands by rubbing them together
Use the 'I Love You' massage: stroke a capital 'I' down baby's left side, then an inverted 'L' across the top and down the left, then a 'U' up the right, across the top, and down the left
Bicycle legs: hold baby's legs and gently cycle them in a pedalling motion for 30–60 seconds
Knee-to-chest: gently press both knees to baby's tummy and hold for 10 seconds, then release
tips: Do this between feeds, not right after, or you may cause spit-up
Use a small amount of baby massage oil if the skin is dry
Warm (not hot) compress on the tummy can be used alongside massage

techniques.name: Baby Carrier / Wrap
techniques.whenToUse: Baby needs constant contact and you need your hands free — great for the 'witching hour' in the evenings
steps: Ensure the carrier is sized correctly and baby is in the TICKS position: Tight, In view, Close enough to kiss, Keep chin off chest, Supported back
Position baby high enough to kiss their head
Ensure hips are in a 'frog' or 'M' position — not dangling straight down
Start with short wear sessions (20–30 min) while you're both learning
Walk around or do light household activities — the motion and your breathing rhythms are very calming
tips: Stretchy wraps (Moby, Solly) are gentler for learning; structured carriers (Ergobaby, Lillebaby) are easier for longer wear
Many fussy evenings can be resolved entirely by babywearing during the 'witching hour' 5–8pm
You cannot spoil a newborn by carrying them — this builds security, not dependence

techniques.name: Warm Bath
techniques.whenToUse: As a wind-down tool before sleep, or to soothe a particularly fussy or tense baby
steps: Fill bath to about 5–8 cm depth; test temperature with your wrist or elbow — should feel warm, not hot (37–38°C)
Support baby's head and neck throughout — never let go
Pour warm water gently over the chest and tummy to keep baby warm
Keep the bath short — 5–10 minutes is enough
Wrap in a warm towel immediately after and do a little skin-to-skin before dressing
tips: A daily bath becomes a strong sleep cue once the routine is established
Some babies find baths stimulating rather than soothing — watch your specific baby's response
Newborns don't need bathing every day — 2–3 times per week is sufficient until the cord falls off

techniques.name: Change of Scene / Fresh Air
techniques.whenToUse: You've tried everything and nothing is working — a complete environment change often breaks the cry cycle
steps: Move from inside to outside, or from one room to another
A short walk with baby in the pram or carrier — even 5–10 minutes
Stand in front of an open window with cool air on baby's face (cool but not cold)
Drive in the car — motion and vibration are highly effective (and you can pull over and stay in the car)
tips: Cool air on the face stimulates the diving reflex and can interrupt a crying escalation
This technique also works for you — stepping outside breaks your own stress response
If nothing is working at 3am, it's okay to put baby safely in the crib and take 5 minutes to breathe
